name: Gullurías Trail
name_original: Cañón del Río Lobos, Senda de las Gullurías y Ermita de San Bartolomé – trail running circular
description: This difficult 6.4-mile (10.3 km) trail running route in Cañón del Río Lobos offers elevated views and leads to the Hermitage of Saint Barth
region_intro: Jogging around Ucero offers routes through varied terrain, characterized by river canyons, dense woodlands, and open landscapes. The region features notable geological formations, including the Cañón del Río Lobos, providing a mix of flat sections and moderate ascents. Elevation gains on running routes typically range from under 100 meters to over 200 meters, presenting options for different fitness levels. The trails often follow riverbeds or wind through forests, offering a natural environment for running. Best jogging routes around Ucero The most popular jogging route is Gullurías Trail, a 6.4 miles (10.3 km) trail that takes 1 hour 21 minutes to complete. This difficult route features varied terrain with an elevation gain of 195 meters. Another top favourite among local runners is Cañón de Casarejos y Cañón del río Lobos – running circular desde Casarejos por el barranco de la Calzada, a difficult 9.0 miles (14.5 km) path. This route explores the canyons of Casarejos and Río Lobos, including a section through the Calzada ravine. Local runners also love the Río Lobos – Hermitage of Saint Bartholomew loop from Ucero, a 14.3 miles (23.0 km) trail leading through the Río Lobos canyon and past the Hermitage of Saint Bartholomew, often completed in about 2 hours 49 minutes. Jogging around Ucero is defined by river canyons, forest paths, and historical landmarks, with routes offering both challenging long-distance running and shorter, more accessible options. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Ucero are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.3 stars from over 3 reviews. More than 120 runners have used komoot to explore Ucero's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Ucero?
A: There are &lt;strong&gt;10 dedicated jogging routes&lt;/strong&gt; around Ucero, offering a variety of experiences for runners. These routes range from moderate to difficult, catering to different fitness levels.

Q: Are there any easy jogging routes for beginners in Ucero?
A: While many routes in Ucero are rated moderate to difficult, there are options that are less strenuous. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38162679" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Saint Bartholomew loop from Comunidad de Herrera de Soria, Nafría de Ucero y Ucero&lt;/a&gt; is a shorter route with a relatively low elevation gain of 52 meters, making it a good choice for those looking for a more accessible run.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the jogging trails in Ucero?
A: Jogging routes in Ucero feature varied terrain, including river canyons, dense woodlands, and open landscapes. You'll encounter notable geological formations, particularly within the Cañón del Río Lobos, with trails often following riverbeds or winding through forests.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in Ucero?
A: Yes, many of the running routes in Ucero are circular. A great example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064201819/canon-de-casarejos-y-canon-del-rio-lobos-running-circular-desde-casarejos-por-el-barranco-de-la-calzada" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cañón de Casarejos y Cañón del río Lobos – running circular desde Casarejos por el barranco de la Calzada&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a challenging loop through two impressive canyons.

Q: What are some long-distance running options around Ucero?
A: For those seeking longer runs, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20150762" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Lobos – Hermitage of Saint Bartholomew loop from Ucero&lt;/a&gt; is a substantial 23 km (14.3 miles) trail. Another excellent choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20150741" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Saint Bartholomew – Boca del Ocejo loop from Parque Natural del Cañón del Río Lobos&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 21.7 km (13.5 miles).

Q: What can I see along the running routes in Ucero?
A: Many running routes offer scenic views and pass by interesting landmarks. You can explore the stunning &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1072360" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Saint Bartholomew&lt;/a&gt;, enjoy panoramic vistas from the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3634900" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gullurías Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, or discover the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1072363" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cueva Grande (Río Lobos Canyon)&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064198016/gullurias-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gullurías Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a great option for combining running with sightseeing.

Q: Are there any viewpoints along the running trails in Ucero?
A: Yes, several running routes offer rewarding viewpoints. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3634900" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gullurías Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; is a notable spot that can be accessed via routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064198016/gullurias-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gullurías Trail&lt;/a&gt;, providing stunning panoramic views of the surrounding landscape.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in Ucero?
A: The running trails in Ucero are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 3.3 stars from over 3 reviews. Runners often praise the diverse natural environment, including the impressive river canyons and forest paths, which offer both challenging long-distance options and more accessible runs.

Q: Are there any trails that explore the Cañón del Río Lobos?
A: Absolutely. The Cañón del Río Lobos is a central feature of the region's running landscape. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064201819/canon-de-casarejos-y-canon-del-rio-lobos-running-circular-desde-casarejos-por-el-barranco-de-la-calzada" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cañón de Casarejos y Cañón del río Lobos – running circular desde Casarejos por el barranco de la Calzada&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20150762" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Lobos – Hermitage of Saint Bartholomew loop from Ucero&lt;/a&gt; both take you through this spectacular canyon.

Q: What is the typical elevation gain on running routes in Ucero?
A: Elevation gains on running routes in Ucero typically range from under 100 meters to over 200 meters. This variety allows for options from relatively flat sections to more challenging ascents, catering to different levels of endurance.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks to see while running in Ucero?
A: Yes, the region is rich in history. A prominent historical landmark is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1072360" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Saint Bartholomew&lt;/a&gt;, which is featured on several running routes, including the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20150762" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Lobos – Hermitage of Saint Bartholomew loop from Ucero&lt;/a&gt;.
tips: Going up to the Gullurías viewpoint will allow us to have a different and higher perspective of the natural park.

Located in a unique setting, isolated at the mouth of the Lobos River Canyon, this beautiful construction was part of a Templar monastery, San Juan de Otero, of which only the chapel remains. The architectural symbols inscribed on its walls have been the source of numerous debates, attributing some to them of esoteric origin.
Near the Lobos river and next to the hermitage of San Bartolomé, we will find this spectacular cave, known as Cueva Grande, from where it is typical to take a spectacular photo of the hermitage.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail is typically accessed from the area around the Hermitage of Saint Bartholomew. Parking is usually available nearby, though it can get busy during peak seasons. It's advisable to arrive early to secure a spot.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginner trail runners?
faqs.answer: While generally considered to have a low to medium difficulty, this trail includes an initial continuous and steep ascent. After this climb, the path tends to flatten out before descending. A good physical condition and appropriate footwear are recommended due to varied terrain, including rocky sections. It's a rewarding challenge for those with some trail running experience.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the Senda de las Gullurías?
faqs.answer: You'll experience diverse terrain, including dense forests of sabines, pine trees, and quejigos, offering shaded sections. There are also rocky parts and areas with aromatic plants like lavender and rosemary. A significant portion of the route provides elevated views of the Cañón del Río Lobos, showcasing impressive limestone cliffs.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: Key highlights include the historic Hermitage of Saint Bartholomew, a 13th-century Templar site. You'll also pass the Gullurías Viewpoint, offering breathtaking panoramic views, and get a unique perspective of the hermitage from View of the Hermitage of San Bartolomé from Cueva Grande. The trail also features ancient kilns and geological formations.

faqs.question: What wildlife might I encounter on the trail?
faqs.answer: The Cañón del Río Lobos is rich in wildlife. You're likely to spot various birds of prey, such as griffon vultures, golden eagles, and Egyptian vultures soaring above the canyon. Other fauna includes wild boars, squirrels, badgers, and otters. The 'Gullurías' in the trail's name refers to the Alondra Totovía, a small lark often seen among the sabines.

faqs.question: Is the trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Cañón del Río Lobos Natural Park generally allows dogs, but they must be kept on a leash at all times to protect the local wildlife and ensure the safety of other visitors. Please remember to clean up after your pet.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to run this trail?
faqs.answer: Spring and autumn are generally the best times to run this trail, offering pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery. Summer can be very hot, especially in exposed sections, so an early start is recommended. Winter can be beautiful but may present slippery conditions if wet, and some sections might be colder.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for the Cañón del Río Lobos Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Typically, there are no entrance fees or specific permits required to access the general areas of the Cañón del Río Lobos Natural Park for activities like trail running. However, it's always a good idea to check the official park website or local information centers for any updated regulations or specific restrictions that might apply during certain seasons or for particular activities.

faqs.question: How does this trail differ from other routes in Cañón del Río Lobos?
faqs.answer: This circular route offers a unique, elevated perspective of the Cañón del Río Lobos, largely avoiding the often-crowded main path along the riverbed. By taking the Senda de las Gullurías, you can enjoy a more tranquil experience of the natural park, especially during peak seasons, while still appreciating its natural beauty and historical sites from a different vantage point.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates significant sections of the Senda de las Gullurías and also passes through parts of the Senda del Río, Senda del Castillo, and briefly touches the GR 160 - Camino del Cid BTT.

faqs.question: Is there a recommended direction to run this circular trail?
faqs.answer: Given the initial continuous and steep ascent mentioned in the trail description, many runners might prefer to tackle this climb early on. Running the trail in a clockwise direction would typically mean getting the most challenging elevation gain out of the way first, allowing for a more gradual descent and flatter sections towards the end. However, both directions offer a rewarding experience.

waypoints.name: View of the Hermitage of San Bartolomé from Cueva Grande
